note: publish.py posts the first item with published=false each run (one per day). Use item['images'] (list) if present else [item['image']]. IG -> carousel (children + carousel container); Bluesky -> image array (max 4). Week 2 (#008-#014) are 4-slide carousels: main slide + 3 explainer slides.

items.title: Why Font feels like glass
items.caption:
Font's glassy slopers weren't polished smooth by climbers. They were hardened millions of years ago.

Long after the Fontainebleau sands were deposited, silica-rich water seeped through the sand and crystallized inside its pore spaces. Where it precipitated, loose sand turned into an almost glass-hard crust — while the rock underneath often stayed softer.

That's why a freshly broken hold in Font can expose plain sand beneath a centimeter of rock-hard skin. And why every broken hold is gone for good.

Source: Fischer & Krause, Klettergeologie, Boulder Mag 02/2026 · Thiry & Maréchal (2001), Journal of Sedimentary Research.

Ever grabbed a Font sloper that felt like porcelain? Which one?

#fontainebleau #bouldering #climbing #geology #sandstone #earthscience #rockclimbing

items.title: Your map is lying to you
items.caption:
Your world map has been lying to you your whole life.

The Mercator projection was built for navigation in 1569: it keeps compass courses straight — but the price is area. The further from the equator, the more the map inflates. Greenland ends up looking like Africa's twin.

In reality: Africa covers about 30.4 million km², Greenland about 2.2 million km². Africa is roughly 14 times larger — the USA, China and India would fit inside it together, with room to spare.

Source: Snyder, Map Projections — A Working Manual (USGS).

Which country's true size surprised you the most?

#maps #cartography #geography #mercator #africa #greenland #dataviz

items.title: Why wet sandstone breaks
items.caption:
Rain turns sandstone back into a sandcastle.

Sandstone is quartz grains glued together by natural cement — silica, carbonate or clay. When water soaks in, clay-bearing cement softens and the rock loses strength: lab studies have measured losses of half or more in some sandstones. Holds that survived millions of years can snap in one damp session.

The tricky part: sandstone dries from the outside in. A hold that looks dry can still be wet where it matters — give the rock time.

In the Elbe sandstone, waiting after rain isn't just good style. It's part of the official Saxon climbing rules.

Source: Hawkins & McConnell (1992), Quarterly Journal of Engineering Geology · Fischer & Krause, Boulder Mag 02/2026.

How long do you wait after rain — 24 hours? 48?

#sandstone #bouldering #climbing #elbsandstein #fontainebleau #climbingethics #geology

items.title: Velvet vs. sandpaper
items.caption:
Same rock type. Opposite skin.

Fontainebleau and Sněžník are both sandstone — but they feel like different planets. Font's quartz sand settled in a calm coastal lagoon: grains of almost equal size, packed smooth. Sněžník sits in the Elbe sandstone, dumped by a powerful Cretaceous delta river: coarse and fine grains mixed, many with fresh, sharp edges.

Geologists call this "sorting" — slow water drops only fine, even grains; strong currents carry everything. Millions of years later, your fingertips can read the difference.

Source: Fischer & Krause, Klettergeologie, Boulder Mag 02/2026.

Team velvet or team sandpaper — where is your skin happier?

#bouldering #climbing #sandstone #fontainebleau #elbsandstein #geology #rockclimbing

items.title: Crystals you can crimp
items.caption:
You're not crimping rock. You're crimping single crystals.

Granite solidifies kilometres below the surface, insulated by the rock above. Cooling takes so long that quartz, feldspar and mica have time to grow into an interlocking mosaic you can see — and grip. Basalt is the opposite story: lava freezing at the surface within days to months, crystals too small for the eye.

The extreme case: obsidian cools so fast that no crystals form at all — natural glass.

Rule of thumb on the wall: the slower the cooling, the coarser the grip.

Source: Winter, Principles of Igneous and Metamorphic Petrology.

Granite, gneiss, limestone or sandstone — what's your favourite rock to climb on?

#granite #basalt #climbing #bouldering #geology #volcano #earthscience

items.title: Your crimps were carved by ice
items.caption:
The best hold-shaper in the mountains? Winter.

Water seeps into every crack and pore. When it freezes, it expands by roughly 9 % — and confined in rock, that expansion builds pressures far beyond what stone can resist. Cycle after cycle, frost levers flakes off walls and carves edges into blank rock.

In the Elbe sandstone, frost shattering likely modelled many of the crimps climbers pull on today — sharp, positive, and geologically brand new.

The flip side: what frost gives, frost takes. Spring is prime time for holds to disappear.

Source: Matsuoka & Murton (2008), Frost weathering, Permafrost and Periglacial Processes · Fischer & Krause, Boulder Mag 02/2026.

Ever come back to a project and found a hold missing?

#climbing #bouldering #geology #frost #winter #elbsandstein #earthscience

items.title: When the Sahara was green
items.caption:
8,000 years ago, you could have paddled across the Sahara.

Between roughly 11,000 and 5,000 years ago — the African Humid Period — a wobble in Earth's orbit (precession, a ~20,000-year cycle) pushed the West African monsoon far north. The world's largest desert turned into savannah with rivers, grazing herds and giant lakes.

The biggest of them, Lake Mega-Chad, covered ~350,000 km² — nearly the size of today's Caspian Sea. Its dusty remains are today's Bodélé Depression, which now fertilizes the Amazon with airborne dust.

Rock art in the central Sahara still shows people swimming.

Source: deMenocal & Tierney, Green Sahara (Nature Education) · Drake & Bristow, Holocene Lake Mega-Chad.

Which "lost world" would you visit first?

#sahara #climate #paleoclimate #geography #africa #earthhistory #greensahara

items.title: Everest's summit is ancient seafloor
items.caption:
The highest rock on Earth is seafloor.

The summit of Mount Everest is made of the Qomolangma Formation: marine limestone deposited in a warm, shallow sea about 450 million years ago, in the Ordovician. When India collided with Asia — starting around 50 million years ago — that seafloor was stacked, folded and pushed to 8,849 metres.

Fun fact: rock samples collected within metres of the summit contain fragments of marine fossils — trilobites, crinoids, ostracods. The top of the world is full of sea creatures.

Source: Myrow et al. (2009), GSA Bulletin · IUGS Geoheritage: The Ordovician rocks of Mount Everest.

Would you rather stand on Everest or send your dream line?

#everest #himalaya #geology #earthhistory #limestone #fossils #mountains

items.title: Why granite splits into perfect cracks
items.caption:
Perfect splitters aren't luck. They're physics.

Granite crystallizes kilometres underground, squeezed by the weight of everything above it. When erosion strips that load away, the rock expands — and cracks. Sheets split off parallel to the surface (exfoliation), while older, roughly right-angled joint sets open into the clean vertical cracks climbers jam.

Fun fact: on Yosemite's domes, these curved sheets peel like onion layers — Half Dome owes its shape to them.

Source: Martel (2017), Journal of Structural Geology · Huber (1987), The Geologic Story of Yosemite National Park.

What's the finest crack you've ever jammed?

#yosemite #cracklimbing #tradclimbing #granite #climbing #geology #splitter

items.title: Tufa: the rock that grows
items.caption:
Most rock erodes away. Kalymnos grew.

Tufa forms when water oversaturated with dissolved calcium carbonate loses CO₂ — the calcite has to come out, and it precipitates layer by layer on walls and overhangs. Drip by drip, the island's caves built their own pipes, blobs and free-hanging stalactites.

Fun fact: that makes tufas younger than the wall they hang on — grown-on holds, delivered after the cliff was finished.

Source: Ford & Pedley (1996), Earth-Science Reviews · BGS: Processes of tufa formation.

Kalymnos: been there, or still on the list?

#kalymnos #tufa #sportclimbing #limestone #climbing #geology #greece

items.title: The Mediterranean was a desert
items.caption:
Six million years ago, the Mediterranean nearly vanished.

Around 5.97 million years ago, tectonics squeezed the gateway to the Atlantic shut. Evaporation beat the rivers, and the sea shrank into hypersaline basins — leaving behind roughly a million cubic kilometres of salt, still buried under the seafloor today. About 5.33 million years ago, Atlantic water broke back through at Gibraltar and the basin refilled — possibly in a catastrophic flood.

Fun fact: some models put the peak of that "Zanclean flood" at a discharge about a thousand times the Amazon — though recent studies argue for a gentler refill. The jury is still out.

Source: Garcia-Castellanos et al. (2009), Nature · Krijgsman et al. (1999), Nature.

What would you rather see: the dry basin or the flood?

#mediterranean #earthhistory #geology #zancleanflood #messinian #earthscience #gibraltar

items.title: Why sandstone turns red
items.caption:
Red rock is mostly not red. It's painted.

The quartz grains in red sandstone are usually pale grey. What makes whole canyons glow is a coating of hematite — iron oxide — wrapped around each grain, often thinner than a human hair. The pigment is a trace by volume, but it dominates everything your eye sees.

Fun fact: much of that colour formed *after* burial, as iron-bearing fluids moved through the sand. A red cliff tells you less about the desert it was born in than about the chemistry that soaked it later.

Source: Walker (1967), GSA Bulletin · Turner (1980), Continental Red Beds.

Favourite red-rock destination — Utah, Todra, or somewhere else?

#sandstone #redrocks #desertclimbing #geology #climbing #moab #earthscience
